stem: Find the multiplicative inverse of \( [[a]] \) modulo \( [[m]] \); that is, the integer \( x \) with \( 1 \le x < [[m]] \) such that \( [[a]]x \equiv 1 \pmod{[[m]]} \).
solution: Since \( [[m]] \) is prime and \( [[a]] \) is not a multiple of it, Fermat's Little Theorem gives \( [[a]]^{-1} \equiv [[a]]^{[[m]]-2} \pmod{[[m]]} \). Computing this residue yields \( x = [[answer]] \), and indeed \( [[a]]\cdot[[answer]] \equiv 1 \pmod{[[m]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( [[a]] \) itself; a number is rarely its own inverse.
This is \( -[[a]] \bmod [[m]] \), the additive inverse, not the multiplicative one.
This is one more than the correct inverse.
This is two more than the correct inverse.
This is \( -[[a]]^{-1} \bmod [[m]] \), the inverse with the wrong sign.
This is three more than the correct inverse.

stem: Solve the linear congruence \( [[a]]x \equiv [[b]] \pmod{[[m]]} \) for the unique integer \( x \) with \( 0 \le x < [[m]] \).
solution: Multiply both sides by \( [[a]]^{-1} \equiv [[a]]^{[[m]]-2} \pmod{[[m]]} \) (Fermat's Little Theorem, since \( [[m]] \) is prime): \( x \equiv [[b]]\cdot[[a]]^{[[m]]-2} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[m]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to multiply by the inverse of \( [[a]] \); it just returns \( b \).
This is the inverse of \( [[a]] \) alone, forgetting to multiply by \( [[b]] \).
This multiplies by \( [[a]] \) instead of by its inverse.
This is one more than the correct solution.
This is two more than the correct solution.
This has the wrong sign relative to the modulus.
This is three more than the correct solution.

stem: The modulus \( [[n]] = [[p]] \cdot [[q]] \) is a product of two distinct primes. Find the multiplicative inverse of \( [[a]] \) modulo \( [[n]] \); that is, the integer \( x \) with \( 1 \le x < [[n]] \) such that \( [[a]]x \equiv 1 \pmod{[[n]]} \).
solution: Since \( [[a]] \) is coprime to \( [[n]]=[[p]][[q]] \), Euler's theorem gives \( [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])}\equiv 1\pmod{[[n]]} \) with \( \varphi([[n]])=([[p]]-1)([[q]]-1) \). So \( [[a]]^{-1}\equiv [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])-1}\equiv [[answer]]\pmod{[[n]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( [[a]] \) itself, not its inverse.
This uses \( p-2 \) as if the modulus were the plain prime \( [[p]] \), not the product \( [[n]] \).
This uses \( q-2 \) as if the modulus were the plain prime \( [[q]] \), not the product \( [[n]] \).
This is one more than the correct inverse.
This is two more than the correct inverse.
This has the wrong sign relative to the modulus.
This is three more than the correct inverse.

stem: Solve the linear congruence \( [[a]]x \equiv [[b]] \pmod{[[m]]} \) for the smallest positive integer \( x \). (Note \( \gcd([[a]],[[m]]) = [[d]] \), which divides \( [[b]] \), so a solution exists.)
solution: Since \( \gcd([[a]],[[m]])=[[d]] \) divides \( [[b]] \), divide the whole congruence by \( [[d]] \) to get the reduced congruence \( [[aprime]]x \equiv [[bprime]] \pmod{[[q]]} \). Since \( [[q]] \) is prime, \( x \equiv [[bprime]]\cdot[[aprime]]^{[[q]]-2} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[q]]} \), the smallest positive solution.
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide the congruence by \( [[d]] \) first, mixing the original modulus with the reduced computation.
This forgets to multiply by the inverse of \( [[aprime]] \); it just returns \( b' \).
This is the inverse of \( [[aprime]] \) alone, forgetting to multiply by \( [[bprime]] \).
This adds a full reduced modulus, giving a solution to the reduced congruence but not the smallest positive one.
This is one more than the correct solution.
This is two more than the correct solution.
This has the wrong sign relative to the reduced modulus.

stem: The modulus \( [[n]] = [[p]] \cdot [[q]] \) is a product of two distinct primes. Solve the linear congruence \( [[a]]x \equiv [[b]] \pmod{[[n]]} \) for the unique integer \( x \) with \( 0 \le x < [[n]] \).
solution: Since \( [[a]] \) is coprime to \( [[n]]=[[p]][[q]] \), Euler's theorem gives \( [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])}\equiv 1\pmod{[[n]]} \) with \( \varphi([[n]])=([[p]]-1)([[q]]-1) \), so \( [[a]]^{-1}\equiv [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])-1}\pmod{[[n]]} \). Multiplying both sides of the congruence by this inverse gives \( x \equiv [[b]]\cdot[[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])-1} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[n]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to multiply by the inverse of \( [[a]] \); it just returns \( b \) reduced mod \( n \).
This is the inverse of \( [[a]] \) alone, forgetting to multiply by \( [[b]] \).
This multiplies by \( [[a]] \) itself instead of by its inverse.
This uses \( p-2 \) as if the modulus were the plain prime \( [[p]] \), not the composite \( [[n]] \).
This is one more than the correct solution.
This is two more than the correct solution.
This has the wrong sign relative to the modulus.

stem: The modulus \( [[n]] = [[p]]^{[[e]]} \) is a prime power. Find the multiplicative inverse of \( [[a]] \) modulo \( [[n]] \); that is, the integer \( x \) with \( 1 \le x < [[n]] \) such that \( [[a]]x \equiv 1 \pmod{[[n]]} \).
solution: For the prime power modulus \( [[n]]=[[p]]^{[[e]]} \), Euler's totient is \( \varphi([[n]]) = [[p]]^{[[e]]-1}([[p]]-1) \). Since \( \gcd([[a]],[[p]])=1 \), Euler's theorem gives \( [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])}\equiv 1\pmod{[[n]]} \), so \( [[a]]^{-1}\equiv [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])-1}\equiv [[answer]]\pmod{[[n]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( [[a]] \) itself, unreduced, not its inverse.
This uses \( p-2 \) as if the modulus were the plain prime \( [[p]] \), not the prime power \( [[n]] \).
This is the naive additive complement \( n-a \), not the multiplicative inverse.
This is one more than the correct inverse.
This is two more than the correct inverse.
This has the wrong sign relative to the modulus.
